content.problemStatement:
Chef has a special interest in studying sets of integers.

Given an integer NNN, he defines a good-set as a set of **distinct non-negative** integers, such that:


The sum of the integers in the set is equal to NNN.
The product of each non-empty subset of the set is **odd**.
Determine the number of **distinct sizes** of good-set that can be formed.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of one integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the number of **distinct sizes** of good-set that can be formed.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^18).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the number of distinct possible sizes of a good‑set whose elements sum to N.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=4, sqrt(4)=2, N even → count of even k ≤2 is 1 (k=2). For N=18, sqrt(18)=4, N even → even k are 2 and 4, giving 2 distinct sizes.
N=1 → only k=1 works (set {1}). N=2 → no k satisfies parity and k^2≤2, so answer 0. N=9 → sqrt=3, N odd → odd k are 1 and 3, so two possible sizes.
N is even, floor(sqrt(N)) = 1000000000, number of even k up to that is 1000000000/2 = 500000000.

solution.approach:
A good‑set can contain only distinct odd positive integers because any even element would make a subset product even. The smallest sum of k distinct odd numbers is 1+3+…+(2k‑1)=k^2. Any larger sum with the same parity can be obtained by repeatedly adding 2 to the largest element, keeping all numbers odd and distinct. Therefore a size k is feasible iff:
1. k^2 ≤ N (the minimal sum fits), and
2. N and k have the same parity (both even or both odd).
Let M = floor(sqrt(N)). All k in [1, M] satisfying the parity condition are valid. The count is simply the number of integers ≤ M with the same parity as N:
- If N is even → count = floor(M / 2) (number of even k).
- If N is odd  → count = ceil(M / 2) = (M + 1) / 2 (number of odd k).
Thus each test case is solved in O(1) time after computing integer sqrt of N.
The integer square root is obtained with built‑in functions (C++/Java/Python) or a binary‑search routine for BigInt in JavaScript.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
